text: The patient is positioned semi-laterally for the adrenalectomy. A fan retractor is inserted through the epigastric port, revealing the right lobe of the liver and the myolipoma adhered to its inferior surface. The gallbladder is visible on the right side. A hook instrument is used to carefully open the peritoneum and separate the myolipoma, approximately fifteen centimeters in size, from the liver.

The surgeon uses a grasper in the left hand to stretch the peritoneum, separating it from the capsule of the lipoma. Blunt dissection and hook dissection are employed to separate the outer peritoneal covering, revealing areolar tissue. Minor bleeding from the liver surface is visible, which is coagulated and cut.

The surgeon stretches the peritoneum to reveal the underlying vena cava, while carefully avoiding the duodenum. The lower edge of the surgical field exposes a portion of the kidney, including the upper pole.

The surgeon identifies the adrenal vein, which is located near the vena cava and renal vein. Suction irrigation is used for blunt dissection, creating a window around the adrenal vein. A Maryland dissector is employed to skeletonize the vein, preparing it for clipping.

The surgeon uses a hook to detach the vein from the myolipoma, creating a window for clipping. A titanium clip is applied using a disposable clip applicator, and an extra clip is removed. The harmonic scalpel is used to separate the myolipoma from the liver's inferior surface, while a fan retractor optimizes traction. Blunt dissection reveals a good areolar plane, allowing for superior mobilization of the lipoma, which is then mobilized inferiorly from the kidney's superior pole.

The surgeon separates the inferior surface of the myolipoma from surrounding muscle tissue, using blunt traction to lift the inferior pole. The assistant holds suction to manage any oozing from the lipoma bed, ensuring clear visibility. The lipoma is positioned lateral to the vena cava and is nearly detached, with only minor superior attachments remaining.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to cut peritoneal tags attached to the lateral wall, while the assistant provides traction to inspect the bed of the lipoma. The lipoma is large and breaks slightly due to suction irrigation traction. The superior pole of the kidney is visible below, and the lipoma is now completely free from superior, medial, and lateral attachments. The lipoma is flipped up, revealing collected blood that is separated using the harmonic scalpel. Blunt dissection is preferred, but the harmonic scalpel is used if bleeding occurs.

The surgeon completes dorsal mobilization of the myolipoma, which is now almost free from surrounding tissues. Below, the kidney is visible, and gentle blunt dissection is used to separate the remaining attachments. An endo bag is introduced to contain the myolipoma, and the mouth of the bag is extruded for manual morcellation using ovum forceps. Suction irrigation checks for any bleeding, and the final attachments are divided with a harmonic scalpel.
